  • Following the Linux device model’smod_devicetable.hstandard
    • MODULE_DEVICE_TABLE(rpmsg, ...) requires a unifiedxxx_device_idformat
  • Allows future field expansion without breaking ABI
  • const char *driver_override

Forces a specific driver name. When set, the bus matching logic bypasses id_table and OF matching, directly comparing the driver name.

Important: The comment says ‘do not set directly’ because the kernel will kfree() this pointer when the device is destroyed. Correct usage:

  • u32 src / u32 dst
FieldMeaningChange Timing
srcLocal address. When the device is created, it may beRPMSG_ADDR_ANY, updated to the actual value after backend allocationrpmsg_dev_probe()inrpdev->src = ept->addr
dstPeer address. Usually a known remote service address, orRPMSG_ADDR_ANYSet by the backend at creation time

src is the address I listen/receive on, dst is who I send to. rpmsg_device represents a logical channel, so it contains both end addresses.

  • struct rpmsg_endpoint *ept

Points to the default endpoint of this channel. When the driver provides a callback,rpmsg_dev_probe() an endpoint is automatically created and assigned here.

Note: An rpmsg_device can only have one default ept, but the driver can manually create additional endpoints in probe() (e.g., when multiple listening addresses are needed).

  • bool announce

Controls whether to send to the remote end when the channel is created/destroyed**Name Service (NS)**announcement message.announce = true: sends “I’m online” on creation, “I’m offline” on destruction. The remote processor can update its service table or trigger corresponding client connections upon receipt.

  • const struct rpmsg_device_ops *ops

Backend operation table, defined inrpmsg_internal.h：

This is the boundary between the RPMSG core layer and the specific backend:

  • rpmsg_core.conly calls these interfaces
  • virtio_rpmsg_bus.c(or other backends) implement these interfaces
  • Allows the RPMSG framework to support multiple underlying transports (though currently mainly virtio)

struct rpmsg_endpoint

endpoint is a first-class object for sending/receiving

rpmsg core attaches all communication operations torpmsg_endpointinstead ofrpmsg_device. This means:

  • Arpmsg_device(channel) can have multiple endpoints.
  • Different endpoints can have different callbacks and addresses.
  • Send operations are performed through endpoints, naturally carrying the source address.

This is similar to the design of TCP sockets: the device is like a socket fd, and the endpoint is like a specific connection endpoint.

rpmsg_rx_cb_tCallback Types

Parameter Details

TypeMeaning
struct rpmsg_device *RPMSG device (channel) that received the message
void *Message data pointer (payload)
intMessage length (payload len)
void *private data (rpmsg_create_eptpassed in whenpriv）
u32sender address

Usually returns 0 to indicate successful processing. The specific meaning is defined by the backend, generally:

  • 0: Message processed, buffer can be released
  • Negative value: processing error

Usage scenarios

  • Scenario A: Simple driver, provide callback during registration

The frameworkrpmsg_dev_probe()automatically creates an endpoint when , calling the backend-implementedrpdev->ops->create_ept()to bind this callback (virtio_rpmsg_bus.c）。

  • Scenario B: Complex driver, manually create multiple endpoints

rpmsg_deviceandrpmsg_endpointrelationship

This is key to understanding the RPMSG architecture:

Relationship summary:

  • 1rpmsg_devicerepresents a logical channel (associated with a remote processor)
  • 1rpmsg_devicehas at least 1 defaultrpmsg_endpoint（rpdev->ept）
  • 1rpmsg_devicecan have N additional endpoints (manually created by the driver)
  • Eachrpmsg_endpointis bound to a uniqueaddr, triggering its own cb when receiving messages sent to that address

Address lifecycle mapping

Stagerpmsg_device->srcrpmsg_endpoint->addrDescription
Device just createdRPMSG_ADDR_ANYNone (not yet created)Waiting for backend allocation
rpmsg_dev_probe()In progressUpdated, set toept->addrept->addrActual address allocated by backend
RuntimeRemains unchangedRemains unchangedUsed for message routing

Key assignment chain:

struct rpmsg_driver

struct rpmsg_driverIt is the core structure that driver developers need to fill in the RPMSG framework. It follows the Linux standard device driver model while encapsulating the RPMSG-specific message sending and receiving semantics.

  • struct device_driver drv

Linux device model base class, used to attach torpmsg_bus

  • const struct rpmsg_device_id *id_table

Device ID table supported by the driver (matched by service name)

  • int (*probe)(struct rpmsg_device *)

Called on successful match, executes driver initialization, refer torpmsg_dev_probefunction

  • void (*remove)(struct rpmsg_device *dev);

Called on device removal/driver unload, performs cleanup

  • int (*callback)(struct rpmsg_device *, void *, int, void *, u32);

Message reception callback (triggers automatic default endpoint creation by the framework),callbackandprobedivision of labor

callback triggers automatic endpoint creation

callbackFramework behaviorApplicable scenarios
non-NULLrpmsg_dev_probe()Automatically creates default endpoint, binds callback torpdev->srcSimple service, single address listening
NULLFramework does not create default endpoint, driver must inprobe()Manual call inrpmsg_create_ept()Complex service, multi-address, dynamic endpoint management

andrpmsg_device / rpmsg_endpointtriangular relationship

ObjectCreated byManaged byLifecycle
rpmsg_deviceBackend (virtio) receives NS messageKernel device modelDuring channel existence
rpmsg_endpointFramework auto-creates (rpdev->ept) or driver manually createskrefReference countBound to device or driver requirements
rpmsg_driverStatic definition by driver authormodule_init / module_exitDuring module loading

Call chain

struct rpmsg_device_ops

Call timing

MemberRequiredCall functionCall timingPrecondition
create_eptRequiredrpmsg_create_ept()① Framework automatically creates default endpoints;
② Driver manually creates endpoints		rpdev->opsNon-empty
announce_createOptionalrpmsg_dev_probe()Driverprobe()After successept created successfully andops->announce_createnon-empty
announce_destroyoptionalrpmsg_dev_remove()At the very beginning of device removal/driver unloadops->announce_destroynon-empty

rpdev->ops->create_ept()

  • Framework auto-createsrpmsg_core.c : rpmsg_dev_probe()
  • Driver manually creates

rpdev->ops->announce_create()

Backend:

  • Send a “channel creation” message to the remote processor via the RPMSG Name Service protocol
  • The message content usually includes: service namerpdev->id.name, local addressrpdev->src

rpdev->ops->announce_destroy()

Backend:

  • Send a “channel destruction” message to the remote end via the name service protocol
  • After receiving it, the remote end will delete the channel from its own service table; subsequent messages sent to that address will be discarded or return an error

Summary

OperationCallerCalleeCore semantics
create_eptrpmsg_create_ept()BackendAllocate resources: bind backend buffer and interrupt to local address
announce_createrpmsg_dev_probe()BackendPublish service: notify remote that “this address has a service listening”
announce_destroyrpmsg_dev_remove()BackendRevoke service: notify remote that “the service at this address is about to stop”

These three hooks together implement the complete lifecycle management of RPMSG “create-publish-revoke”, serving as the most critical contract interface between the core layer and the backend

struct rpmsg_endpoint_ops

Decoupling of rpmsg core and backend

The architecture of the rpmsg subsystem can be viewed as two layers:

LayeropsOperation objectTypical operation
Rpmsg Devicerpmsg_device_opsrpmsg_device (channel)Create endpoint, announce channel existence
Rpmsg Endpointrpmsg_endpoint_opsrpmsg_endpoint(communication endpoint)Send data, destroy endpoint, poll

This layering allows the core to perform name service announcement during device registration (via rpmsg_device_ops.announce_create), while the actual data transmission goes through the endpoint (via rpmsg_endpoint_ops.send）。

Hierarchical relationship:

Forwarding logic of rpmsg core

drivers/rpmsg/rpmsg_core.cEach API in it is a thin layer of encapsulation, taking a few typical functions as examples:

Because send is required, the core has no fallback. If XIO.

Seerpmsg_core.cAlthough the comment says sendto is optional, the semantics of optional are “the backend can choose not to support this operation,” not “the core will handle compatibility for you.” If a backend only implements send and trysend, calling sendto will fail.

virtio_rpmsg_bus.cdoes not implement poll, so throughrpmsg_poll()returns 0 when called. Anddrivers/rpmsg/rpmsg_char.c

rpmsg_char.cprovides user-space/dev/rpmsgXinterface.rpmsg_poll()The result is ORed intopoll maskIf the backend does not implement poll, user-space operations on/dev/rpmsgXdoingpoll()can only detect read events (EPOLLIN), and cannot throughrpmsg_poll()Check the write-ready status. However, due to virtio’srpmsg_sendseries either blocking or returning immediately, the write path does not actually rely on poll.

Summary

opsrequiredCore behavior (when not implemented)virtio backend
destroy_ept✅ requiredN/A (will not be NULL, must be set during initialization)virtio_rpmsg_destroy_ept
send✅ required-ENXIOvirtio_rpmsg_send
trysend✅ required-ENXIOvirtio_rpmsg_trysend
sendtooptional-ENXIOvirtio_rpmsg_sendto
send_offchanneloptional-ENXIOvirtio_rpmsg_send_offchannel
trysendtooptional-ENXIOvirtio_rpmsg_trysendto
trysend_offchanneloptional-ENXIOvirtio_rpmsg_trysend_offchannel
polloptionalReturns 0Undefined

EXPORT_SYMBOLS

rpmsg_create_ept()

Callrpdev->opsincreate_ept

rpmsg_destroy_ept()

rpmsg_send()

rpmsg_sendto()

rpmsg_send_offchannel

rpmsg_trysend()

rpmsg_trysendto()

rpmsg_poll()

rpmsg_trysend_offchannel()

rpmsg_find_device()

Complete flowchart

Click the code block to expand
entry.summary: This article introduces the bus abstraction mechanism of Rpmsg Core in Linux 5.10.23 and the access method of its backend, and discusses in detail the matching and scoring rules between devices and drivers as well as the device probing process. It summarizes core logic such as power domain association, automatic endpoint creation, and dynamic address allocation during probing, revealing the implementation details of the rpmsg framework in hardware power management and endpoint communication.

entry.title: Virtio Rpmsg Bus
entry.content:
Timeline

Timeline

2026-06-22

init

This article introduces the implementation principles and core data structures of the virtio-based rpmsg bus driver in the Linux kernel. It discusses the basic communication model of rpmsg and provides a detailed analysis of the rpmsg_device、rpmsg_endpoint creation and destruction logic, as well as the underlying transport mechanisms such as virtqueue send/receive channels, buffer management, DMA mapping, and concurrency control within the virtproc_info private state.

linux 5.10.238

rpmsg Structure

rpmsg has three layers:

virtio_rpmsg_bus.cIn the middle and lower layers, its responsibilities are:

  1. Take over the virtio rpmsg device
  2. Use vring to send and receive messages
  3. Create/destroy rpmsg_device based on name service

It producesrpmsg_device, but does not consume. The consumer isrpmsg_driver。

module_init/module_exit

virtio_rpmsg_bus.cis the Linux kernel’svirtio-based rpmsg bus driver. Its role is:

Enable the Linux main core and remote processor to send and receive rpmsg messages via the virtio vring mechanism, and abstract the remote service into a Linuxrpmsg_device, for upper-layer rpmsg driver binding and use.
This file itself is thevirtio transport implementation of the rpmsg bus, it does not care about specific business protocols, such as audio, sensors, TEE, MCU control, etc.; it is only responsible for delivering messages to the corresponding endpoint.

Basic Communication Model

rpmsg

The communication unit of rpmsg is:

  • rpmsg_devicerpmsg channel device, a communication channel, is a “device”
  • rpmsg_driverThe driver that handles the business of this channel
  • rpmsg_endpointThe actual sending and receiving endpoints on the channel (address + callback)

Relationship among the three:

rpmsg_device is the middle “device managed by the driver”. When creatingrpmsg_device, the probe function is triggered for matching, and upon successful matching, therpmsg_driver->probefunction is called

struct rpmsg_device

wherestruct rpmsg_deviceDefined as follows:

struct rpmsg_device_opsrepresents arpmsg_deviceoperation

An rpmsg_device is equivalent to a logical channel, for example:

  • channel name: “rpmsg-demo”
  • src: local endpoint address
  • dst: remote endpoint address

Invirtio_rpmsg_bus.c, the channel is created like this (rpmsg_create_channel()）：

That is: the remote announces a service “rpmsg-demo”, Linux creates arpmsg_devicerepresenting this channel, or the local side actively creates a service “rpmsg-demo”, i.e., creates arpmsg_devicerepresenting the local channel. After announcement, the NS service on the remote side also creates a channel for “rpmsg-demo”. The local and remoterpmsg_devicehave src and dst exactly opposite, and communicate through this channel.

In fact,virtio_rpmsg_bus.cthe abstract rpmsg channel descriptor in is

i.e., arpmsg_deviceAlso add the core data structurestruct virtproc_info *vrp, which is the key data structure for virtio to implement the rpmsg bus

struct rpmsg_endpoint

rpmsg_deviceJust like a telephone line + an extension number service, whilerpmsg_endpointrepresents the person actually answering the phone

The logic related to rpmsg_endpoint is mainly inrpmsg_core.c, among which the most important arerpmsg_rx_cb_t cbandconst struct rpmsg_endpoint_ops *opsthese two members:

This function represents the callback triggered when the ept receives a message

represents the operation function of rpmsg_endpoint.

Endpoint creation

The core function is:__rpmsg_create_ept(), which is the operation function of rpmsg_device

  1. Allocatestruct rpmsg_endpoint
  2. Initialize reference count and callback lock
  3. Record callback, private data, ops
  4. Assign local address to endpoint
  5. Insertvrp->endpointsthis idr

Key logic:

  • If the caller does not specify an address, dynamically allocate
  • Dynamic addresses start from1024start
  • 0 ~ 1023reserved for predefined services
    While reserved address definition:

Endpoint address viaidr_alloc()allocation:

Endpoint destruction

Core function:__rpmsg_destroy_ept(), it is the operation function of rpmsg_endpoint

  1. fromidrdelete endpoint from
  2. set callback toNULL
  3. decrease reference count, release endpoint if necessary

key code:

pay special attention to concurrency here:

  • delete idr: prevent new RX messages from finding this endpoint
  • setcb = NULL: prevent in-flight RX that already obtained the endpoint from calling callback
  • kref: prevent release while RX path is using the endpoint

struct virtproc_info

core data structure:struct virtproc_info

this is the private state of the entire virtio rpmsg device, attached tovdev->priv = vrp, divided by function

  • virtio basics:
    • vdevunderlying virtio device
  • TX/RX channels:
    • rvq, svq RX/TX virtqueue
  • Buffer management:
    • rbufs, sbufsRX/TX buffer virtual address
    • num_bufsTotal buffer count
    • buf_sizeSingle buffer size
    • last_sbufTX pioneer cursor
    • bufs_dmaBuffer DMA base address
  • Transmission synchronization:
    • tx_lockProtect svq/sbufs/sleepers
    • sendqWait queue for TX buffers
    • sleepersWaiter count (controls tx-complete interrupt)
  • Endpoint management:
    • endpointsEndpoint idr (lookup by address)
    • endpoints_lockProtect endpoint table
  • name service：
    • ns_eptName service endpoint (addr 53)

struct virtio_device *vdev

Purpose: points to the underlying virtio device. It is the root of this rpmsg instance. Through it, you can obtain:
- vdev->dev device node (dev_err/dev_for debugging)
- vdev->config virtio configuration operations (reset/del_vqs, etc.)
- vdev->priv points back to vrp itself
Set in probe:

In callback, it relies on this to retrieve vrp:

struct virtqueue *rvq, *svq

Purpose:

  • rvq = receive virtqueue: remote -> Linux
  • svq = send virtqueue: Linux -> remote
    These are the two channels for receiving and sending. In probe:
  • Receiving messages:virtqueue_get_buf(rvq) / virtqueue_add_inbuf(rvq)
  • Sending messages:virtqueue_add_outbuf(svq) / virtqueue_get_buf(svq)

void *rbufs, *sbufs

Purpose:

  • rbufs: starting kernel virtual address of the RX buffer area
  • sbufs: starting kernel virtual address of the TX buffer region

In probe, split a whole DMA memory block into two halves:

as shown in the figure

get_a_tx_buf()That is, get the TX buffer from sbufs by index:

unsigned int num_bufs

Purpose: total number of RX + TX buffers (each half). Calculated in probe based on vring size:

Therefore:

Used in many places asnum_bufs / 2as a boundary, for exampleget_a_tx_buf()：

unsigned int buf_size

Purpose: byte size of a single buffer, currently fixed at 512 (MAX_RPMSG_BUF_SIZE）。

In the probe function

It determines:

  • address stride of each buffer:sbufs + buf_size * i
  • upper limit of a single message payload

int last_sbuf

Function: The “pioneer cursor” of TX buffer, recording how many have been initially allocated in order. Inget_a_tx_buf()used in:

  • last_sbuf < num_bufs/2: If there are unused new TX buffers, take them in order
  • Otherwise: All TX buffers have been used, switch to recycling from the used ring
    Once the upper limit is reached,last_sbufit stops growing and relies entirely onvirtqueue_get_buf()recycling.

dma_addr_t bufs_dma

Function: The DMA base address of the entire buffer (address from device/DMA perspective). In probe, it isdma_alloc_coherent()returned by:

rbufs/sbufsis the virtual address used by CPU, bufs_dma is the physical/bus address used for release and DMA mapping. Use it to release during remove:

struct mutex tx_lock

Function: Protects shared state on the sending side: svq, sbufs, sleepers. Allows multiple senders to call concurrentlyrpmsg_send(). A mutex is used because sending may need to wake up a remote processor that is “napping”, and this process may sleep, so a sleepable mutex must be used.

struct idr endpoints

Function: The idr index table for all local endpoints, with the key being the endpoint address. Used to quickly find an endpoint by address. This is the core data structure for message distribution. When creating an endpoint, allocate an address and insert:

When receiving a message, look up by dst:

So rpmsg is actuallyaddress-based dispatch, it relies on this idr.

struct mutex endpoints_lock

Purpose: Protect concurrent access to the endpoints idr. Must hold when adding, deleting, querying, or modifying endpoints:

Note that it and tx_lock are two different locks:
- tx_lockProtects the send path
- endpoints_lockProtects the endpoint table

wait_queue_head_t sendq

Purpose: Senders waiting for a TX buffer sleep on this wait queue. When there is no TX buffer, rpmsg_send() sleeps and waits:

After the remote side consumes the TX buffer, the TX complete callback wakes it up:

atomic_t sleepers

Purpose: How many senders are currently waiting for a TX buffer (waiter count). It works with the dynamic toggling of the TX complete interrupt:

Design purpose:

  • No one waiting for TX buffer: Disable tx-complete interrupt to save overhead
  • Someone waiting for TX buffer: Enable tx-complete interrupt, wake up immediately after remote returns buffer

(This is the design of version 5.10. The Linux 7.1.1 version has removed sleepers due to the introduction of poll support.)

struct rpmsg_endpoint *ns_ept

Purpose: dedicated endpoint for name service (fixed address 53). It does not belong to any ordinary rpmsg channel; it is used internally by the bus to handle remote service ‘create/destroy’ notifications. It is created in probe when the remote side supports the NS feature:

Its callback isrpmsg_ns_cb(), responsible for creating/destroying rpmsg_device according to NS messages. It is destroyed separately during remove:

Note that rpdev is passed as NULL during creation because it is not bound to a specific channel.

Buffer Design

The driver uses fixed-size buffers. Related definitions:

  • Up to 512 buffers
  • Half RX, half TX
  • Each buffer is 512 bytes
  • Maximum total memory: 512 * 512 = 256 KiB

That is:

Allocated in probe:

Then split:

Each sent message has a common header:

Meaning:

FieldMeaning
srcSource endpoint address
dstDestination endpoint address
reservedReserved
lenPayload length
flagsMessage flags
data[]Actual business data

After Linux receives the message, it willdstfind the local endpoint by the address, and then call the callback of that endpoint.

get_a_tx_buf()

Code:

This function is a very simple TX buffer allocator.

It has two phases:

last_sbufOnly useful in the first phase.

Once:

Then just keep going:

That is to say:

virtio API

APIFunction
virtio_find_vqs()Find/Create virtqueue
virtqueue_add_inbuf()Give the device a “writable” buffer
virtqueue_add_outbuf()Give the device a “readable” buffer
virtqueue_add_sgsGeneric buffer addition function includes in and out
virtqueue_get_buf()Retrieve the buffer processed by the device from the used ring
virtqueue_kick()Notify the other side: the queue has a new buffer
virtqueue_kick_prepare()Determine whether notification is needed
virtqueue_notify()Actually send the notification
virtqueue_enable_cb()Enable virtqueue callback/interrupt
virtqueue_disable_cb()Disable virtqueue callback/interrupt, used to prevent interrupt storms during polling
virtqueue_get_vring_size()Get ring size
virtio_has_feature()Check virtio feature
virtio_device_ready()Set DRIVER_OK, the device can start working

probe function

virtio_driver.probeThe function is defined as:

Analysis as follows

Allocatevirtproc_info

Initialize:

Find virtqueue

The driver needs two virtqueues:

Then:

Two queues:

Meaning:

virtqueuePurposecallback
rvq / inputLinux receives messages from the remote endrpmsg_recv_done()
svq / outputLinux sends a message to the remote endrpmsg_xmit_done()

Calculate the number of buffers

That is: if the vring is small, reduce the number of buffers according to the vring capacity; otherwise, at most 512 buffers

Allocate coherent DMA buffer

This buffer is shared DMA memory accessible by both the main core and the virtio backend.

Pre-place RX buffers into the RX virtqueue

This step is crucial:

Linux first places a batch of empty RX buffers into the available ring, so that the remote processor can later write messages into these buffers.

Disable TX complete interrupt by default

Because most of the time, the sender does not need to receive an interrupt every time a TX buffer is consumed by the remote end. Only when Linux has no TX buffers and the sender needs to sleep and wait, is the TX complete interrupt temporarily enabled.

Create name service endpoint

If the remote end supports:

Then create a name service endpoint with address53:

Address definition:

This endpoint is specifically for handling remote service creation/destruction notifications.

Device ready and notify remote end

The order here is important:

  1. RX buffer is ready (virtqueue_kick_prepare)
  2. Set virtio device to ready
  3. Notify remote end that it can start sending messages

Finally print:

remove function

Procedure:

  1. reset virtio device

First stop the device to avoid further sending and receiving.

  1. Delete all child rpmsg devices

Inrpmsg_remove_device()call:

  1. Destroy NS endpoint
  1. endpoints、virtqueue、DMA buffer、vrp

Send message (rpmsg_send_offchannel_raw())

All send APIs eventually enterrpmsg_send_offchannel_raw()

The call chain is roughly:

Parameter check

First check src/dst:

Broadcast addresses are not allowed as actual send addresses.
Then check the length:

Because a single buffer is fixed at 512 bytes, the payload cannot exceed512 - sizeof(struct rpmsg_hdr)

Get TX buffer

Its logic:

Meaning:

  1. Initial stage: directly take an unused buffer from the TX buffer pool
  2. Subsequent stage: from thesvqused ring, reclaim TX buffers that have been consumed by the remote end

What to do when there is no TX buffer?

If it istrysend：

If it is normalsend, then wait, up to 15 seconds:

If timeout:

Before waiting, it will call:

Function: If this is the first sender sleeping and waiting for a TX buffer, enable the TX complete interrupt

After waiting ends, it will call:

If there are no more waiters, disable the TX complete interrupt:

Fill the rpmsg header and payload

Note that here we usecpu_to_virtio16/32(), because virtio devices may have specific endianness requirements.

Add to the TX virtqueue and kick the remote side

Then:

Full meaning:
Linux fills the TX buffer → adds the buffer to the output virtqueue → kicks the remote processor → the remote side takes the message from the virtqueue

TX complete callback

Set in the probe function

That is, the TX callback function isrpmsg_xmit_done

Note: Normally, the TX complete interrupt is disabled; it is only enabled when a sender is sleeping and waiting for a buffer.

When the remote end consumes the TX buffer, the virtio backend places the buffer into the used ring and triggers the TX complete callback. Linux wakes up the sending thread waiting for the TX buffer.

TX sleepers mechanism:rpmsg_upref_sleepers()

Meaning: Before a sending thread prepares to sleep due to lack of TX buffer:sleepers++, if this is the first waiter, enable the TX complete callback
Correspondingly, there is alsorpmsg_downref_sleepers(): After the sending thread wakes up:sleepers--, if this is the last waiter, disable the TX complete callback

Purpose of this mechanism:

  • No one waiting for TX buffer: disable TX complete interrupt to reduce overhead
  • Someone waiting for TX buffer: enable TX complete interrupt to immediately wake up the waiter when the remote end returns the buffer

Complete chain:

Receive message (rpmsg_recv_done)

Set in the probe function

That is, the rx callback function isrpmsg_recv_done, the function is defined as follows:

rpmsg_recv_done

Take used buffer from RX virtqueue

If a msg is obtained, try to continue and loop through all available messages:

Single message processing:rpmsg_recv_single()

When receiving, it is not distributed by channel name, but by:msg->dst, invrp->endpointslook up the local endpoint. So rpmsg actually runs more like address-based messaging, and the channel name is mainly used for device discovery and driver matching.
Main steps:

  1. Parse payload length
  1. Check if the message length is valid

Prevent out-of-bounds caused by abnormal length from the remote end.

  1. According todstfind the endpoint

This is the core of rpmsg distribution:msg->dst == 本地 endpoint 地址

  1. Increase endpoint reference count

Prevent endpoint from being released during callback execution.

  1. Call callback

Parameters passed to the upper-layer callback include:

ParameterMeaning
ept->rpdevCorresponding rpmsg device
msg->datapayload
msg_lenPayload length
ept->privEndpoint private data
msg->srcRemote source address
  1. Put RX buffer back into virtqueue
    After processing, this buffer needs to be reused by the remote side:

Kick remote

Finally, inrpmsg_recv_done()If a message has been processed inside, kick the remote end:

Name service mechanism

Note: name service is an optional feature. Feature definition:

If the remote end does not support this feature, dynamic service discovery is unavailable. In this case, the channel may need to be created statically.

rpmsg supports the remote end dynamically notifying Linux: I created a service or I destroyed a service, this is called name service. Related structure:

Fields:

FieldMeaning
nameService name
addrRemote service address
flagsCreate or destroy

flags：

NS endpoint

Name service uses a fixed address:

Created during probe:

Note hererpdevThe parameter isNULL, because the NS endpoint does not belong to a regular rpmsg channel, but is an internal endpoint used by the bus itself.

NS callback：rpmsg_ns_cb()

After receiving a remote NS message:

  1. Check length
  2. Ensure the endpoint is not bound to a realrpdev
  3. Fix the end of the name string
  4. Constructrpmsg_channel_info
  5. Create or destroy a channel based on flags

Create channel:

Destroy channel:

Local service announce mechanism

This driver can not only receive remote NS, but also announce local services to the remote end.

Related functions:

When a local rpmsg channel that needs to be announced is created, the driver constructsrpmsg_ns_msg, and sends it to the remote NS address53(atrpmsg_core.c). Create notification:

Destroy notification:

rpmsg channel creation

It, based on the incomingstruct rpmsg_channel_info *chinfocreate arpmsg_device。

Process:

  1. Check if the same channel already exists

If it already exists, do not create it again.

  1. Allocatevirtio_rpmsg_channel

this structure:

It is a virtio transport private channel, exposing therpmsg_device。

  1. Fillrpmsg_device

Then register:

After registration, the rpmsg bus will match the id table of the upper-layer rpmsg driver, and then call the probe of the corresponding driver.

Overall send/receive flow diagram

Linux sends a message to the remote end

Linux receives a message from the remote end

Remote end publishes a service

RX buffer lifecycle

RX buffer lifecycle:

Therefore, the RX buffer is cyclically reused.

TX buffer lifecycle

TX buffer lifecycle:

Reference documentation

[1]
AMPRelated：3 Linux rpmsgSubsystem(STM32MP157Solution)
[2]
Multi-core heterogeneous inter-core communication–ipcc
[3]
Remote Processor Messaging (RPMsg)
entry.summary: This article introduces the implementation principles and core data structures of the virtio-based rpmsg bus driver in the Linux kernel. It discusses the basic communication model of rpmsg and provides a detailed analysis of the rpmsg_device、rpmsg_endpoint creation and destruction logic, as well as the underlying transport mechanisms such as virtqueue send/receive channels, buffer management, DMA mapping, and concurrency control within the virtproc_info private state.

entry.title: Common Data Structures in the Linux Kernel
entry.content:
Timeline

Timeline

2026-05-24

init

This article introduces the core data structures frequently used in the Linux 6.x kernel, detailing the definitions, core APIs, and typical usage scenarios of structures such as doubly circular linked lists, hash lists, red-black trees, radix trees, extensible arrays, and priority lists.

Overview

The Linux kernel defines a large number of general-purpose data structures that permeate various subsystems such as process scheduling, memory management, file systems, and device drivers. This article organizes them byactual usage frequencyfrom high to low, covering data structure definitions, core APIs, and typical usage scenarios.

info

This article is based on theLinux 6.xkernel; some APIs may vary slightly between versions.

Basic Containers

list_head — Doubly Circular Linked List

**Usage frequency: Highest.**This is the most widely used data structure in the Linux kernel, bar none. Almost every subsystem uses it.

success

Linux’s linked list implementation separatesSeparation of data and linked list nodes, the linked list node is embedded into the structure, rather than the structure containing a linked list pointer. The essence of this design is that a single set of linked list operations applies to all data types.

Header file: <linux/list.h>

Data structure:

Core API:

APIDescription
LIST_HEAD(name)Statically define and initialize a list head
INIT_LIST_HEAD(ptr)Dynamically initialize a list head
list_add(new, head)Insert after head
list_add_tail(new, head)Insert before head (tail)
list_del(entry)Delete a node
list_del_init(entry)Delete and reinitialize a node
list_empty(head)Check if the list is empty
list_entry(ptr, type, member)Get the containing structure from a list_head pointer
list_for_each(pos, head)Traverse the list
list_for_each_entry(pos, head, member)Traverse the linked list and obtain the host structure
list_for_each_entry_safe(pos, n, head, member)Safe traversal (allows deletion during traversal)
list_move(list, head)Move a node to a new linked list
list_splice(list, head)Merge two linked lists

Usage example:

Run

list_addandlist_add_tail:

list_add_tailare inserted intohead—>prevandheadbetween. However, because this is a ring, “before head” is logically equivalent to “the end of the linked list”.

list_delandlist_del_initfunction

list_replacecan replace a linked list node

hlist — hash linked list

hlistis specifically designed forhash tablea doubly linked list variant. That is, when hashing the data to be stored, if a conflict occurs, usethe linked list methodto store the conflicting data. Typically, the order of element usage in a hash table is: data storage —> data retrieval —> data deletion. Compared tolist_headthe difference is that the head node uses only onestruct hlist_head(single pointer), saving memory in the hash table array.

Its core design motivation is to solve the standard doubly circular linked listlist_headwhen used as a hash bucket, the existingmemory wasteandsemantic mismatchproblems.

Featureslist_head(Standard Linked List)hlist_head + hlist_node(Hash Linked List)
Head Node StructureFull Bidirectional Pointers (next, prev)Only One Unidirectional Pointer (first)
Data Node StructureBidirectional Pointers (next, prev)Bidirectional Pointers (next, pprev)
Is CircularYes (Head and Tail Connected)No (Ends with NULL)
Empty List Checkhead->next == headhead->first == NULL
Memory Overhead (Head Node)2 Pointers (16 bytes/64-bit)1 pointer (8 bytes/64 bits)

Header file: <linux/list.h>

Data structure:

pprevThe type isstruct hlist_node **(pointer to pointer).
It does not store the “address of the previous node”, but**“the memory address of the next field in the previous node”**。

For the hlist chain head -> A -> B

  • For node B in the middle of the list:B->pprev == &(A->next)
  • For node A in the list:A->pprev == &(head->first)

This design facilitates deletion

Add related

rbtree — Red-Black Tree

the most used in the kernelself-balancing binary search tree, providing O(log n) search, insertion, and deletion. Each node of a red-black tree has a storage bit indicating the node’s color, which can be red or black. Properties of red-black trees:

  • Each node is either black or red.
  • The root node is black.
  • Every leaf node (NIL) is black. [Note: Here, leaf nodes refer to empty (NIL or NULL) leaf nodes!]
  • If a node is red, then its children must be black.
  • All paths from a node to its descendant nodes contain the same number of black nodes. This property ensures that no path is more than twice as long as any other, thus the red-black tree is a relatively balanced binary tree.

All operations on a red-black tree must preserve its properties. Red-black trees are widely used, mainly for storing ordered data, with a time complexity of O(log n), making them highly efficient. cfs_rq uses a red-black tree to store tasks.

Header file: <linux/rbtree.h> / <linux/rbtree_augmented.h>

Data structure:

At first glance, there seems to be no field for color, but this is a clever aspect of the red-black tree implementation here.__rb_parent_colorThis field actually contains both the color information and the parent node pointer. Since the field is of type long and requires alignment of sizeof(long), on typical 32-bit machines, the last two bits are always 0, so one of those bits can be used to represent the color.

The key lies inmemory alignment：

  • struct rb_nodeis forced to be aligned tosizeof(long)alignment.
  • On a 32-bit system,sizeof(long) == 4, meaning any validrb_nodepointer address must be amultiple of 4, i.e., the lowest2 bitsare always00。
  • On a 64-bit system,sizeof(long) == 8, the lowest3 bitsare always000。

In fact, the lowest bits are used here to represent color information. The following operations on the parent pointer and color information are essentially operations on therb_parent_colorbits.

Linux’s red-black tree implementation is optimized for speed, thus having one less indirection layer (better cache locality) than traditional implementations. Eachstruct rb_nodeinstance of the structure is embedded in the data structure it manages, so there is no need to separate them with pointersrb_nodeand the data structure it manages.

  • Users should write their own tree search and insertion functions to call the provided red-black tree functions, rather than using a comparison callback function pointer.

  • Locking code is also left for the red-black tree user to write。

Example

radix_tree — Radix Tree

xarraypredecessor, used for mapping integer IDs to pointers. Althoughxarrayhas gradually replaced it, there is still a large amount of code in the kernel using radix trees.

For new projects, please directly use<linux/xarray.h>XArray API in . XArray fixes the radix_tree’s many design flaws (such as preload complexity, index offset issues), and the API is more concise. radix_tree in 5.10.x is merely a compatibility layer on top of XArray.

Header file: <linux/radix-tree.h>

visiblelinux-5.10.xit has been replaced by xarray in

spinlock_t — spin lock

The most basic of the Linux kernelbusy-wait lock, used for protecting short critical sections in SMP systems. When the holder spins on one CPU, executors on other CPUs also spin.

Header file: <linux/spinlock.h>

Data structure (simplified):

Core API:

APIDescription
spin_lock_init(lock)Dynamic initialization
DEFINE_SPINLOCK(lock)Static definition + initialization
spin_lock(lock)Acquire lock (disable kernel preemption)
spin_unlock(lock)Release lock
spin_lock_irq(lock)Acquire lock and disable local interrupts
spin_unlock_irq(lock)Release lock and enable local interrupts
spin_lock_irqsave(lock, flags)Acquire lock, save interrupt state
spin_unlock_irqrestore(lock, flags)Release lock, restore interrupt state
spin_lock_bh(lock)Acquire lock and disable bottom half
spin_trylock(lock)Try to acquire lock (non-blocking)
spin_is_locked(lock)Check lock status
warning

While holding a spinlockmust never sleep(cannot callkmalloc(GFP_KERNEL)、copy_from_userand other potentially blocking operations). This is one of the most common sources of bugs in the kernel.

Selection guide:

ScenarioAPI
Between process contextsspin_lock / spin_unlock
Between process and interruptspin_lock_irqsave / spin_unlock_irqrestore
Between different interruptsspin_lock_irqsave / spin_unlock_irqrestore
Between process and bottom halfspin_lock_bh / spin_unlock_bh

mutex — mutual exclusion lock

Unlike spinlocks,mutexWhen the lock cannot be acquired, it yields the CPU and goes to sleep, suitable forcritical sections that may sleep。

Header file: <linux/mutex.h>

Data structure:

Core API:

APIDescription
mutex_init(lock)Dynamic initialization
DEFINE_MUTEX(lock)Static definition
mutex_lock(lock)Acquire lock (may sleep)
mutex_unlock(lock)Release lock
mutex_lock_interruptible(lock)Acquisition interruptible by signals
mutex_trylock(lock)Try to acquire (non-blocking)
mutex_is_locked(lock)Check status
warning

mutexThe locker must be responsible for unlocking (lock/unlock in different contexts is not allowed). The kernel strictly checks this.

Choice between spinlock and mutex:

spinlockmutex
When unable to acquireSpin-waitSleep and yield CPU
Critical sectionShort (nanoseconds)Can be longer (milliseconds)
Can sleep?Absolutely notYes
Interrupt contextAvailableNot available
System overheadLowHigher (involving scheduling)

completionis implemented in the kernelA synchronization mechanism where one thread waits for another to complete a task, which is lighter than semaphores and has clearer semantics.

RCU (rcu_head) — Read-Copy-Update

RCU (Read-Copy-Update) is an importantlock-free synchronization mechanism, suitable for read-mostly scenarios. Readers are completely lock-free; writers copy first, then update, and wait for all readers to finish before reclaiming old data.

Header file: <linux/rcupdate.h> / <linux/srcu.h>

Data Structure:

Core API:

APIDescription

rcu_read_unlock()Reader leaves critical section
call_rcu(head, func)Register reclamation callback
synchronize_rcu()Wait for all readers to complete (blocking)
rcu_assign_pointer(p, v)Writer updates pointer
rcu_dereference(p)Reader dereferences pointer
kfree_rcu(ptr, rcu_field)RCU safe memory reclamation

Typical Pattern:

Typical Kernel Applications:

  • Network routing table lookup
  • Filesystem dentry cache
  • radix_tree / xarrayLockless lookup
  • fdtableExtension of (file descriptor table)

Network

sk_buff(often abbreviated asskb) is the Linuxnetwork subsystem’s core data structure, representing a network packet. It runs through the entire network stack: from NIC driver reception to application layer transmission, all usesk_buffas the carrier.

Linux：KernelhashTable——hlist
[3]
LinuxRed-Black Tree in（rbtree）
[4]
Linux：radixImplementation Analysis
entry.summary: This article introduces the core data structures frequently used in the Linux 6.x kernel, detailing the definitions, core APIs, and typical usage scenarios of structures such as doubly circular linked lists, hash lists, red-black trees, radix trees, extensible arrays, and priority lists.

entry.content:
Timeline

Timeline

2026-05-24

init

This article introduces the download, installation, and editor integration methods of Claude Code, detailing how to configure and use it with the DeepSeek API in VSCode and Emacs. Additionally, it discusses Claude Code's Skill extension mechanism, the MCP model context protocol, as well as the installation of related Skills and recommended plugins.

ClaudeCode

Download Claude Code, requires Node.js

DeepSeek

Choose DeepSeek here because its tokens are cheaper and don’t require an external network connection. Click the link below to apply for an API key

DeepSeek Open Platform

https://platform.deepseek.com/api_keys

Editor Integration

Vscode

Download the plugin:Claude Code for VS CodeAnthropic anthropic.com, then find it in the settings@ext:Anthropic.claude-codeunderClaudeCode: Environment VariablesClickEdit in settings.json

Refer to the official documentation to set environment variables

IntegrationClaude Code

https://api-docs.deepseek.com/zh-cn/quick_start/agent_integrations/claude_code

As follows:

After that, open the Claude Code plugin in VSCode and it will be ready to use.

You can also set environment variables in Windows PowerShell. First, open PowerShell with administrator privileges, then

Then write the environment variables from the official documentation, so that these environment variables are loaded every time PowerShell is opened.

Emacs

Emacs is generally used in Linux environments; Windows Emacs has too many bugs, so the following configuration is all for the Linux environment. First, still install Claude Code, then set the environment variables.$HOME/.bashrcor$HOME/.zshrc

Then enable the environment variables

Mainly use the claude-code.el package:

claude-code.el

https://github.com/stevemolitor/claude-code.el

The configuration is as follows:

EnterC-c c cto start the Claude Code window. Other commands can be viewed via claude-code-transient (C-c c m).

Skill

Skill is an extension mechanism for Claude Code launched by Anthropic in October 2025. It can be understood as a skill pack for Claude Code, essentially a Markdown file.SKILL.mdIt contains the purpose, prompts, rules, and workflow of this Skill. Its storage location:

  • Project-level directory, which is under your project root directory${workspaceFolder}/.claude/skills/Skills placed here only take effect in the current project, suitable for skills strongly tied to the project, such as project-specific code conventions, deployment processes, etc.
  • User-level directory, which is the directory under your home folder$HOME/.claude/skills/Skills placed here are available in all projects, suitable for general skills unrelated to specific projects, such as making PPTs, writing documents, formatting WeChat public accounts, etc.

Another concept is MCP (Model Context Protocol). MCP is a standard protocol that allows AI assistants to safely interact with the external world. For example, enabling AI to read local files on your computer, operate GitHub repositories, query databases, send Slack messages, control browsers, etc.

Install Skills as follows:

  1. Manual copySKILL.mdto the project-level directory or user-level directory
  2. Install plugins

Reference:

Discover and install pre-built plugins through the marketplace

https://code.claude.com/docs/zh-CN/discover-plugins

The official Anthropic marketplace (claude-plugins-official) is automatically available when starting Claude Code. Run /plugin and go to the Discover tab to browse available content, orclaude.com/pluginsview the directory.

Recommended Skills to install

Karpathy-Inspired Claude Code Guidelines

https://github.com/multica-ai/andrej-karpathy-skills

codegraph:

codegraph

draw.io

draw.io

https://github.com/jgraph/drawio-mcp/blob/main/plugins/claude-code/README.md

CC-Switch

A very useful AI aggregation tool that allows easy switching between different Agents and Models

With CC-Switch, there’s no need to set environment variables

Additionally, it discusses Claude Code's Skill extension mechanism, the MCP model context protocol, as well as the installation of related Skills and recommended plugins.

entry.title: smatch
entry.content:
Timeline

Timeline

2026-05-21

init

This article introduces Smatch, a static analysis tool designed specifically for the Linux kernel, detailing its compilation method, database construction process, and its ability to discover hidden bugs through cross-file analysis. Additionally, it summarizes the specific steps for running Smatch to perform static analysis on kernel and non-kernel projects, along with the configuration methods for related script parameters.

Smatch is a static analysis tool designed specifically for the Linux kernel, capable of deeply tracing code execution paths to uncover deeply hidden bugs, such as null pointer dereferences, lock usage errors, resource leaks, etc. Reference documentation:

smatch

https://github.com/error27/smatch/blob/master/Documentation/smatch.rst

Compile Smatch

kernel

Smatch essentially follows the kernel build system, so it must be able to correctly locate the compiler and header files. Therefore, if cross-compilation is needed, environment variables must be set first.

Analyze and Build Database

build_kernel_data.shwill append--call-tree, --info, --spammy, --data=$DATA_DIRparameter callsmatch_scripts/test_kernel.sh, then callsmatch_data/db/create_db.shbuild Smatch database

Smatch does not require building a database, but building one makes it more powerful. Once the database is built, the next time you compile, Smatch can look up information such as ‘how many times the printk function is called throughout the kernel’ or ‘whether a certain struct pointer has been freed in other C files,’ enabling epic cross-file vulnerability detection.

Run smatch for static analysis of the kernel: Run smatch to check the entire kernel

Script parameters

  • --endian: Enable big/little endian byte order checking.
  • --target "bzImage": Specify the build target (default is bzImage modules).
  • --log smatch_compile.warns: Specify the build log output file
  • --wlog smatch_warns.txt: Customize the output file name for Smatch warnings.

After compilation, two files will be generated in the current directory:

  • smatch_compile.warns: Complete kernel build log (including errors and warnings).
  • smatch_warns.txt: Pure Smatch static check warning messages.

You can also check code under a specific file or folder

The smatch author recommends frequently updating the smatch database, as each update makes cross-function checks more accurate

When running, smatch reads some rules set in smatch_data, in~/repository/linux/smatch-1.74/smatch.cmiddlestatic char *get_data_dir(char *arg0)there is related logic: you can pass--data=/path/to/smatch_dataSpecify

out-of-tree module

For non-kernel projects, use the following method to build the database and analyze

  • --file-outputOutput one result per file
  • --succeedIndicates to continue analysis even if errors occur
  • --call-treeIndicates enabling cross-function call analysis
  • --infoIndicates more detailed analysis logs
  • --spammyIndicates more aggressive checking

When the --file-output parameter is used, smatch will not print warnings directly to the terminal’s stdout/stderr by default, but will instead generate a bunch of hidden text files in the same directory as your code:

  • .hello_world.c.smatch(General warnings)
  • .hello_world.c.smatch.caller_info(If you added parameters like --info)
    Static analysis

References

[1]
Automatic detection Linux Wrong tool - Static section
[2]
Kernel Testing Guide
entry.summary: This article introduces Smatch, a static analysis tool designed specifically for the Linux kernel, detailing its compilation method, database construction process, and its ability to discover hidden bugs through cross-file analysis. Additionally, it summarizes the specific steps for running Smatch to perform static analysis on kernel and non-kernel projects, along with the configuration methods for related script parameters.

entry.title: LeetCode Hot 100 P1143 Longest Common Subsequence
entry.content:
Timeline

Timeline

2026-03-21

init

Dynamic Programming

Problem:

P1143 Longest Common Subsequence

https://leetcode.cn/problems/longest-common-subsequence/description/?envType=study-plan-v2&envId=top-100-liked

It’s a classic problem,dp[i][j]denotestext1[0..=i]andtext2[0..=j]the length of the longest common subsequence, the state transition equation is

  • When text1[i]==text2[j] text1[i] == text2[j] text1[i]==text2[j]:
    • dp[i][j]=dp[i−1][j−1]+1 dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1 dp[i][j]=dp[i−1][j−1]+1
  • otherwise:
    • dp[i][j]=max(dp[i−1][j],dp[i][j−1]) dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]) dp[i][j]=max(dp[i−1][j],dp[i][j−1])
entry.summary: Dynamic Programming

entry.title: LeetCode Hot 100 P416 Partition Equal Subset Sum
entry.content:
Timeline

Timeline

2026-03-21

init

Dynamic Programming

Problem:

P416 Partition Equal Subset Sum

https://leetcode.cn/problems/partition-equal-subset-sum/?envType=study-plan-v2&envId=top-100-liked

Sum the subarray, then divide the sum by 2, transform to find a subset of the array that sums to half of the total, which is a 0-1 knapsack problem

dp[i][j] indicates whether there exists a selection of some positive integers (possibly zero) from the subarray [0,i] such that the sum of selected positive integers equals j. Initially, all elements in dp are false.

Initialization:

  • If no positive integer is selected, the sum of selected positive integers is 0. Therefore, for all 0 ≤ i < n, dp[i][0] = true.
  • When i==0, there is only one positive integer nums[0] that can be selected, so dp[0][nums[0]] = true.

State transition:

  • dp[i][j] = dp[i−1][j] ∣ dp[i−1][j−nums[i]] (j >= nums[i])
  • dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] (j < nums[i])
entry.summary: Dynamic Programming
